abstract: This article investigates how far-right actors on Instagram leverage positive affect, humour, and memetic content to normalise extremist worldviews. Conceptualising far-right Instagram as an 'affective-discursive ecology,' the authors combine algorithmic ethnography with multimodal discourse analysis of 2,603 Reels featuring far-right adjacent themes. The study examines how platform logics, ideology, and emotion intersect to facilitate mainstreaming and mobilisation of extremist content.
key_claims: Far-right communication on Instagram employs positively valenced affect and humour as strategies to normalise extremist worldviews.
Far-right Instagram constitutes an 'affective-discursive ecology' in which ideology, emotion, and platform logics are mutually constitutive.
Multimodal softening through memes and humorous Reels facilitates the mainstreaming of far-right ideas.
Algorithmic proximity on Instagram contributes to the visibility, circulation, and mobilisation of far-right content.
methods: Algorithmic ethnography of Instagram to trace the circulation and recommendation of far-right adjacent content.
Multimodal discourse analysis of audiovisual, textual, and affective dimensions of Reels.
Analysis of a dataset of 2,603 Reels featuring far-right adjacent themes.
findings: Far-right Instagram content relies on positive affect and humour rather than overt hostility to soften extremist messaging.
Memetic and multimodal forms function as vehicles for normalisation and mainstreaming of far-right ideology.
Platform algorithmic logics produce proximity between mainstream and far-right adjacent content, supporting mobilisation.
contributions: Introduces the concept of 'affective-discursive ecology' to theorise far-right communication on Instagram.
Bridges literatures on mainstreaming, memetic communication, and affective publics with platform and algorithm studies.
Provides a large-scale multimodal empirical analysis of far-right Reels, an under-studied format.
Demonstrates a methodological combination of algorithmic ethnography and multimodal discourse analysis for studying extremism on visual platforms.
framing: The paper positions itself at the intersection of research on the mainstreaming of the far right, memetic and affective communication, and critical platform studies. It contributes to debates about how visual social media platforms enable the normalisation of extremism through everyday, emotionally resonant content rather than overtly hostile rhetoric.
